steps.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale-of-5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.

What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?

steps.text: Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

steps.text: You've read bar graphs before. Here's the same Playground data as a bar graph.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Scale of 1 used 40 symbols for Olivia. Scale of 2 used 20. Scale of 5 used just 8 — the fewest. The bigger the scale number, the fewer symbols you need. Click Scale of 5.

params.description: Data table appears. Weekend Screen Time (minutes). Saturday: 35, Sunday: 30, Monday: 40, Tuesday: 25. Horizontal layout.

steps.text: This bar graph is horizontal — the bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s. What comes after 35 on this axis?
What comes after 35 on this axis?

validator.description: Student selected 40 — correct

steps.text: 40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways.

validator.description: Student selected 36 — counting by ones instead of recognizing the skip-counting pattern

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Look at all the numbers on the axis: 0, 5, 10, 15. They're not going up by 1 — what pattern do you see?

validator.description: Student selected 45 — skipped ahead instead of finding the next number in sequence

steps.text: Here's a hint: 45 is on the axis, but it comes later. What number sits between 35 and 45?

validator.description: Student selected 30 — went backwards on the axis instead of forward

steps.text: You're getting there. 30 comes before 35 on the axis. The question asks what comes after — which number is next?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Axis labels light up in sequence: 30, 35, 40

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. The axis counts by 5s. After 30 comes 35. After 35 comes 40. See how that works?

params.description: System sets all bars to correct lengths: Saturday 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I read each value from the table—Saturday 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25—then set each bar to match. Now you see the complete graph.

steps.text: How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?
How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?

validator.description: Student selected 65 — correct

steps.text: 65 minutes in all. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted.

validator.description: Student selected 5 — confused by the scale; counted jumps instead of reading values

steps.text: You're working on it. Each jump is 5 minutes. Saturday's bar goes to 35, not 5. Count by 5s to find each day's total.

validator.description: Student selected 35 — read Saturday's value only, didn't add Sunday

steps.text: Here's a hint: 35 is Saturday's minutes. The question asks for Saturday AND Sunday IN ALL—you need to add both days together.

validator.description: Student selected 70 — added Saturday and Sunday but made a calculation error (35+30=65, not 70)

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Saturday is 35 minutes, Sunday is 30. When we add them: 35 plus 30 equals 65, not 70.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Saturday bar (35) and Sunday bar (30) pulse in sequence, then combined total of 65 appears

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Saturday's bar reaches 35 minutes. Sunday's bar reaches 30 minutes. To find the total IN ALL, we add them: 35 plus 30 equals 65 minutes. The answer is 65.

elements.description: Completed horizontal bar graph. Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph from earlier (Books Read) appears alongside bar graph.

steps.text: You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?
What do these graphs have in common?

tool.name: multiple_choice
options: They both use the same scale

condition.selected: They both use the same scale

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use the same scale' — correct

steps.text: Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols — count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Weekend Screen Time.
Horizontal picture graph showing Books Read This Month.

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use symbols' — incorrect because bar graphs use bars, not symbols

validator.description: Student selected 'They look the same' — incorrect because the graphs have different visual formats

steps.text: Let's think about this together. These graphs look different—one uses symbols, one uses bars. But check the numbers on the side of each graph.

validator.description: Student selected 'They both have 5 categories' — incorrect because each graph has 4 categories

steps.text: You're working on it. Count the categories—both graphs have 4, not 5. The number 5 appears somewhere else on both graphs.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Scale marker pulses on picture graph showing each symbol = 5
Scale numbers pulse on bar graph axis showing 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Look at the picture graph—each symbol equals 5. Now look at the bar graph—the numbers go up by 5. Both graphs use the same scale of 5, just in different ways.
